episode.title: Cariboo Signals - Arts, Culture & Digital Storytelling
episode.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Arts, Culture & Digital Storytelling.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [NYT Business] James Murdoch’s Company Said to Be in Talks to Acquire Major Parts of Vox Media and [Engadget] Spotify now lets AI agents like OpenClaw generate personal podcasts, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: How can rural cultural institutions build sustainable, discoverable storytelling capacity in an environment flooded with AI-generated content while maintaining community control and authentic relationships? Topics include: AI-generated content's impact on discoverability for authentic rural cultural storytelling, Governance models balancing community control with visibility (Mukurtu tiered-access system), Funding models for digital capacity at small cultural institutions (provincial arts funding vs. co-op models), Technology's relationship to community vitality (downstream not upstream), Place-specific authenticity as defense against synthetic content and media pressure.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Museum of the Cariboo Chilcotin

discussion.central_question: How can rural cultural institutions build sustainable, discoverable storytelling capacity in an environment flooded with AI-generated content while maintaining community control and authentic relationships?
discussion.riley_position: Emerging technology infrastructure and emerging tools create opportunities for cultural institutions to position themselves strategically now, particularly for transmitting embodied knowledge remotely. Provincial funding or cooperative models for dedicated digital capacity at anchor institutions can change trajectories meaningfully over time.
riley_key_evidence: The reggae band case study where authentic relationships survived AI-diluted streaming through live shows and Bandcamp direct channels
Motion-capture exoskeleton technology demonstrating potential for transmitting embodied craft knowledge remotely across distance
Canadaland's word-of-mouth discovery model within defined professional communities rather than broad algorithms
discussion.casey_position: The structural bottleneck isn't technology costs but sustained editorial capacity and institutional funding, which are unlikely to materialize from traditional sources. Communities should optimize for depth of relationship with existing audiences rather than reach, and recognize that authentic, place-specific content's value lies in its irreplaceability for local audiences, not commercial scalability.
casey_key_evidence: The Métis beadwork revival came through in-person workshops and elder teaching, not digital distribution tools
Small Cariboo cultural institutions operate with minimal digital budgets (under $10k annually) and thin governance bandwidth
Atlantic humanities funding cuts demonstrate arts budgets are first to be eliminated in austerity cycles
discussion.resolution: The hosts converged on optimizing for depth over scale and rooting digital documentation in existing community practices (live events, gatherings, cultural ceremonies) rather than pursuing algorithmic reach. They acknowledged the maintenance gap persists and remains difficult to solve, but agreed inaction in a hostile content environment is worse than pursuing imperfect solutions.
topics_covered: AI-generated content's impact on discoverability for authentic rural cultural storytelling
Governance models balancing community control with visibility (Mukurtu tiered-access system)
Funding models for digital capacity at small cultural institutions (provincial arts funding vs. co-op models)
Technology's relationship to community vitality (downstream not upstream)
Place-specific authenticity as defense against synthetic content and media pressure
